learning_objectives: Navigate the Blender 4 viewport with confidence using keyboard shortcuts and mouse controls
Understand 3D coordinate systems and the relationship between world, object, and local space
Configure the Blender workspace for efficient modeling workflows
Use fundamental selection, transformation, and navigation controls fluently
readings: Blender Fundamentals (Blender Studio official tutorials, Sections 1-4)
The Art of 3D Computer Animation and Effects by Isaac Kerlow, Ch. 1-2
Blender 4.0 Release Notes: Interface Changes

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to 3D Modeling and Animation, a hands-on course that takes you from the fundamentals of polygon modeling through texturing, rigging, and animation to a portfolio-ready final project. Over the next fifteen weeks, you will learn to create game-ready 3D assets in Blender that meet the technical constraints of real-time rendering while achieving the artistic quality expected in professional studios. Blender has evolved from a niche open-source tool to an industry-standard application used by studios from Ubisoft to Netflix. Its non-destructive modeling workflow, integrated sculpting tools, PBR material system, and robust animation pipeline make it a complete 3D content creation suite. This course focuses on the game asset pipeline specifically: low-poly modeling with clean topology, efficient UV layouts, PBR texturing for real-time rendering, rigging for real-time animation, and export to game engine formats. Every model you create must work in a game engine, not just look good in a render.
key_concepts: 3D coordinate systems: X (red), Y (green), Z (blue) in Blender's right-handed coordinate system with Z-up convention
Viewport navigation: orbit (MMB), pan (Shift+MMB), zoom (scroll), numpad views (1=front, 3=right, 7=top, 5=toggle perspective/ortho)
Object mode vs Edit mode: scene-level manipulation (position, rotation, scale) versus mesh-level manipulation (vertices, edges, faces)
Workspace layouts: Modeling, Sculpting, UV Editing, Shading, Animation -- switching between optimized panel arrangements
Selection modes: vertex, edge, face selection in Edit mode, box select (B), circle select (C), lasso select (Ctrl+RMB)
Transform operations: grab (G), rotate (R), scale (S) with axis constraints (G+X, G+Shift+Z), precise values by typing numbers
Pivot points: individual origins, 3D cursor, median point, active element -- each changes how transforms are applied to selections
Blender's undo system: Ctrl+Z for undo with configurable history depth, Edit > Preferences > System for memory allocation
Viewport shading modes: wireframe, solid, material preview, rendered -- and their uses during different workflow stages
Add-on ecosystem: essential add-ons for game development (TexTools, UV Toolkit, Rigify for quick rigging)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Guided tour of the Blender 4 interface: customizing the workspace layout for modeling, creating a simple prop (wooden crate) starting from a cube, entering Edit Mode, practicing grab/rotate/scale with axis constraints, using extrude (E) and inset (I) to add detail, and rendering a quick preview in Material Preview mode with HDRI lighting
setup_requirements: Blender 4.0+ installed (free from blender.org)
Three-button mouse strongly recommended (or emulation configured)
Reference image collection for modeling exercises (provided)
16GB RAM minimum, dedicated GPU recommended (NVIDIA or AMD with 4GB+ VRAM)

review.value: Object Mode manipulates entire objects (position, rotation, scale in world space); Edit Mode manipulates individual vertices, edges, and faces within a single mesh
Blender uses a right-handed coordinate system with Z-up: X is left-right (red), Y is front-back (green), Z is up-down (blue)
Four display modes from fastest to most accurate: Wireframe (topology only), Solid (basic shading), Material Preview (PBR with HDRI), Rendered (full render engine)
The center of rotation and scaling operations; changing between Individual Origins, Median Point, and 3D Cursor enables different transform behaviors for multi-object editing

learning_objectives: Apply box modeling workflow to create both hard-surface and organic forms
Use extrude, inset, loop cut, and bevel tools proficiently for shape refinement
Understand subdivision surface workflow and its requirements for clean base geometry
Model a complete hard-surface prop meeting game-ready polygon budget constraints
readings: Blender Documentation: Mesh Modeling Tools
Pushing Points: A Guide to Topology by William Vaughan, Ch. 1-3
Game Art Complete by Andrew Gahan, Ch. 4 (Hard Surface Modeling)

lecture_notes.introduction: Box modeling is the foundational technique of 3D modeling: start with a primitive shape (usually a cube) and progressively refine it through extrusion, cutting, and reshaping until it matches your reference. The name comes from the starting point, not the result -- box modeling produces everything from mechanical props to organic characters. The workflow is inherently iterative: rough out the major forms first, then add detail in successive passes. This matches how sculptors work in clay and how concept artists work in sketch. The discipline is knowing when to add detail and when to stop. Every polygon you add costs performance in a game engine, so the skill is not in adding detail but in suggesting detail with minimal geometry. A well-modeled game asset looks complex but is built from surprisingly few polygons placed with precision. Today we master the core modeling tools and build a complete hard-surface prop from start to finish.
key_concepts: Box modeling philosophy: start simple (primitive), rough form (major proportions), refine (add edge loops, adjust vertices), detail (bevel, inset for small features)
Extrude (E): extending faces outward to build new geometry, with normal, region, and individual extrude modes
Inset (I): creating a new face within an existing face, essential for adding detail and preparing for extrusion on specific areas
Loop Cut (Ctrl+R): adding edge loops that follow the mesh topology, controlling density and supporting subdivision
Bevel (Ctrl+B): chamfering edges to add controlled roundness, with segment count and profile shape for hard-surface detail
Subdivision Surface modifier: smoothing geometry by subdividing each face into four, requiring clean quad topology for predictable results
Mirror modifier: modeling only half of symmetric objects with real-time mirroring, saving time and ensuring perfect symmetry
Boolean operations: union, difference, intersection for mechanical parts, with cleanup requirements for resulting topology
Proportional editing (O): soft-selection falloff for organic shape adjustment, with different falloff curves (smooth, sphere, sharp)
Reference images: setting up front and side reference images as background in the viewport for accurate modeling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Box modeling a sci-fi crate prop: starting from a cube, adding loop cuts for panel lines, insetting faces for recessed details, beveling edges for industrial roundness, using the Mirror modifier for symmetry, adding mechanical details (hinges, latches) with extrude and boolean operations, and comparing the result at subdivision levels 0, 1, and 2 to understand how clean geometry subdivides predictably
setup_requirements: Blender with reference images loaded as background (provided: sci-fi crate concept art)
Modeling workspace configured with properties panel visible
Grid snapping enabled for precise dimension control

review.value: An iterative modeling technique starting from a primitive shape, progressively refined through extrusion, loop cuts, and vertex adjustment to match a target form
A modifier that smooths geometry by subdividing each face into four smaller faces, requiring clean quad topology to produce predictable, artifact-free results
A chamfer operation that replaces sharp edges with angled faces, controlled by width and segment count, essential for hard-surface modeling where real objects never have infinitely sharp edges
A non-destructive modifier that mirrors geometry across a chosen axis in real time, enabling symmetric modeling while only editing one half

learning_objectives: Evaluate mesh topology quality and identify common topology problems
Design edge flow that supports animation deformation at joints and facial features
Resolve topology problems: n-gons, triangular poles, non-manifold geometry, flipped normals
Apply topology rules to create meshes that subdivide cleanly and deform predictably
readings: Topology Guides (topologyguides.com): Face Loops, Edge Flow, and Common Topology Patterns
Blender Documentation: Mesh Analysis Tools
Stop Staring: Facial Modeling and Animation Done Right by Jason Osipa, Ch. 2 (Facial Topology)

lecture_notes.introduction: Topology is the invisible architecture of a 3D model. Two models can look identical in a still render but behave completely differently when animated -- one deforms smoothly at every joint while the other collapses into twisted geometry. The difference is topology: how vertices, edges, and faces are connected. Good topology follows rules that ensure predictable behavior under subdivision, deformation, and rendering. Quads subdivide cleanly; triangles create pinching artifacts; n-gons triangulate unpredictably and must be avoided in deformation areas. Edge loops must flow in the direction of movement: around the eyes for blinking, around the mouth for speech, around joints for bending. Today we learn to see topology as the structural engineering of 3D models -- the part that is invisible to the viewer but determines whether the model works in production. These principles apply regardless of the software you use and will serve you throughout your career in 3D.
key_concepts: Quad-dominant topology: four-sided faces subdivide predictably into four quads, deform smoothly, and render consistently across all angles
Edge loops: continuous chains of edges forming rings around the mesh, defining form and enabling clean deformation along their axis
Face loops: continuous strips of faces that follow the surface flow, important for UV unwrapping and texture painting
Poles: vertices where more or fewer than 4 edges meet; 3-poles (triangular) and 5-poles (pentagonal) redirect edge flow and must be placed strategically
N-gon problems: faces with 5+ sides must be internally triangulated by the renderer, causing unpredictable shading and deformation artifacts during animation
Edge flow for deformation: loops must follow the direction of movement -- concentric around eyes, parallel around elbows and knees, radial around shoulders
Non-manifold geometry: edges shared by more than 2 faces, faces with zero area, disconnected vertices -- all cause rendering and export errors
Normal direction: face normals must point outward consistently; flipped normals appear inside-out in game engines that use backface culling
Mesh analysis overlay: enabling Blender's mesh analysis display to visualize distortion, sharp angles, and non-manifold elements
Retopology workflow: creating clean low-poly topology over a sculpted high-poly reference using the Shrinkwrap modifier or RetopoFlow add-on
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing and fixing topology on a character head model: identifying n-gons around the ear that will deform badly, replacing them with quad rings; tracing edge loops around the eyes and mouth to verify they form complete rings; placing strategic 5-poles to redirect edge flow from the cheek into the nose bridge; checking for non-manifold edges with Select > All by Trait > Non-Manifold; and demonstrating the before/after difference by posing the face with shape keys
setup_requirements: Blender with provided character head model (two versions: good topology and bad topology for comparison)
Mesh Analysis overlay enabled (Viewport Overlays > Mesh Analysis)
Topology reference sheets (provided: face topology, body topology, hand topology)

review.value: Continuous chains of edges forming rings around a mesh, critical for defining form, supporting subdivision, and ensuring clean deformation -- must follow the direction of intended movement
Mesh construction using predominantly four-sided faces that subdivide predictably, deform smoothly, and avoid the pinching and shading artifacts produced by triangles and n-gons
Vertices where the number of connecting edges differs from 4; 3-poles and 5-poles redirect edge flow and must be placed in non-deforming areas to avoid animation artifacts
Mesh errors including edges shared by 3+ faces, zero-area faces, and disconnected vertices that cause rendering errors, export failures, and physics calculation problems in game engines

learning_objectives: Understand UV space and the mathematical relationship between 3D surfaces and 2D texture coordinates
Mark seams strategically to minimize visible texture discontinuities
Unwrap meshes with minimal distortion using multiple unwrap methods
Pack UV islands efficiently to maximize texture resolution utilization
readings: Blender Documentation: UV Mapping
Texturing & Modeling: A Procedural Approach by Ebert et al., Ch. 2
Game Art Tricks: UV Optimization for Real-Time Assets (80.lv article)

lecture_notes.introduction: UV unwrapping is the process of flattening a 3D surface into 2D so that textures can be painted onto it accurately. Think of it as peeling the skin off an orange and pressing it flat -- where you cut determines how the flat pieces relate to the original surface. In game development, UV quality directly impacts texture resolution: poorly packed UVs waste texture space, inconsistent texel density creates visible quality differences across the model, and badly placed seams create visible discontinuities that break visual immersion. UV unwrapping is often cited by artists as the least glamorous part of the 3D pipeline, but it is one of the most impactful. A beautifully sculpted model with bad UVs will look worse in-game than a simpler model with excellent UVs. Today we master the strategies and tools for creating production-quality UV layouts that maximize texture quality while hiding seam placement from the viewer.
key_concepts: UV coordinates: U (horizontal) and V (vertical) axes mapping 2D texture space (0-1 range) to 3D surface points, independent of mesh resolution
Seam placement strategy: place seams along natural material boundaries (clothing edges, belt lines), in hidden areas (inner arms, behind ears, under shoes), and along hard geometric edges
Unwrap methods: Smart UV Project (quick automatic), Unwrap with manual seams (most control), Project from View (flat surfaces), Cube/Cylinder/Sphere projection (matching primitives)
UV distortion checking: applying a checker texture to verify even texel density; stretched checkers indicate low-density areas needing more UV space
UV island packing: arranging unwrapped islands within the 0-1 UV space to minimize wasted area, maintaining consistent texel density across all islands
Island margins: leaving pixel-gap margins between UV islands to prevent texture bleeding at mipmap levels, typically 2-4 pixels at the target resolution
Texel density: the number of texture pixels per unit of 3D surface area; consistent density ensures uniform visual detail across the entire model
Overlapping UVs: intentionally stacking symmetric UV islands to share texture space (mirrored left/right arms sharing one UV area)
UDIM tiles: multi-tile UV layouts that span beyond the 0-1 range for high-resolution texturing of large assets, commonly used for film but increasingly for games
UV editing tools: pin vertices to lock solved positions, Live Unwrap for interactive feedback, Relax for reducing distortion, Pack Islands for automatic layout
lecture_notes.demonstration: UV unwrapping a game character: marking seams along clothing boundaries (shirt sleeve edge, waistband, boot top), along hidden areas (inner arm seam, hairline, shoe sole), unwrapping with Live Unwrap enabled for interactive feedback, checking distortion with a 512x512 checker texture, adjusting island sizes for consistent texel density (face gets proportionally more UV space than boots), packing islands with 4-pixel margin for 2048x2048 target resolution, and pinning critical vertices to stabilize the layout during adjustments
setup_requirements: Blender with UV Editing workspace layout
Checker texture for distortion testing (provided: 512x512 and 1024x1024)
Completed character model from previous sessions with clean quad topology

review.value: The process of projecting a 3D mesh surface onto a 2D plane, creating a flat layout (UV map) that defines how 2D textures wrap around the 3D geometry
The number of texture pixels (texels) per unit of 3D surface area; consistent texel density ensures uniform detail level across the entire model, verified with checker textures
Pixel-width gaps between UV islands that prevent texture color bleeding at lower mipmap levels, typically 2-4 pixels for 2048x2048 textures
Strategic positioning of UV cuts along natural material boundaries, hidden areas, and hard geometric edges to minimize visible texture discontinuities

learning_objectives: Create PBR texture sets using the metallic/roughness workflow in Substance Painter
Understand each PBR texture channel and the physical properties it encodes
Bake high-poly detail onto low-poly game meshes using normal maps and AO
Export texture sets optimized for game engine import
readings: The PBR Guide by Allegorithmic/Adobe, Parts 1 and 2
Substance Painter Getting Started Guide (Adobe documentation)
Blender Documentation: Shader Editor and Principled BSDF

lecture_notes.introduction: Texturing transforms gray geometry into believable surfaces -- a metal pipe that gleams under light, a wooden crate with worn edges, a stone wall with moss in its cracks. Physically-based rendering (PBR) provides a consistent, predictable framework where material properties match real-world physics, ensuring that textures look correct under any lighting condition. Before PBR, artists had to manually paint highlights and shadows into textures, which looked correct only under the specific lighting they were painted for. PBR separates material definition (what is this surface made of?) from lighting calculation (how does light interact with it?), allowing a single texture set to work in sunny outdoor scenes, dark dungeons, and neon-lit cities without modification. Today we learn the full PBR texturing pipeline: baking high-poly detail onto low-poly meshes, painting materials in Substance Painter, and exporting optimized texture sets for game engines.
key_concepts: PBR texture channels: Base Color (surface color without lighting), Metallic (0 = non-metal, 1 = metal), Roughness (0 = mirror, 1 = matte), Normal (surface detail as tangent-space vectors), AO (contact shadows), Emissive (self-illumination)
Metallic vs specular workflow: metallic is simpler with a binary metal/non-metal map; specular provides more control but is more complex and less intuitive
Normal map baking: transferring sculpted detail from a high-poly model to a low-poly game mesh by encoding surface angles as RGB colors in tangent space
AO baking: pre-computing ambient occlusion (contact darkness in crevices) as a texture for subtle shading detail without runtime cost
Smart Materials in Substance Painter: procedural material presets that adapt to mesh curvature, position, and ambient occlusion for realistic weathering
Hand-painting in Substance Painter: using brush tools to add unique details, damage, and storytelling elements that procedural materials cannot provide
Texture resolution budgeting: 4096x4096 for hero assets, 2048x2048 for standard props, 1024x1024 for background objects, 512x512 for distant or small items
Channel packing: combining grayscale textures into a single RGB image (R=Metallic, G=Roughness, B=AO) to reduce texture count and memory usage
Texture atlasing: combining multiple objects' textures onto a single sheet to reduce draw calls in game engines
Export presets: configuring Substance Painter export for specific engines (Godot, Unity, Unreal) with correct channel naming and format
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full texturing pipeline for a game prop: importing the low-poly crate model and high-poly sculpt into Substance Painter, baking normal map, AO, curvature, and position maps, applying a Smart Material for base wood and metal surfaces, hand-painting wear on edges using curvature-based generators, adding rust and dirt with procedural fill layers masked by position and AO, painting unique markings and labels by hand, and exporting the complete texture set (Base Color, ORM channel-packed, Normal, Emissive) for Godot import
setup_requirements: Substance Painter (educational license or trial version)
UV-unwrapped low-poly model and matching high-poly sculpt from previous sessions
Reference images for material study (provided: wood, metal, fabric, stone)
Alternative: Blender Texture Paint mode for students without Substance Painter access

review.value: A shading model approximating real-world light interaction using measurable material properties (metalness, roughness), ensuring consistent appearance across all lighting environments

Capturing surface detail from a high-polygon sculpt and encoding it as tangent-space normal vectors in an RGB texture applied to a low-polygon game mesh, faking geometric detail without additional vertices
Combining multiple grayscale texture maps into the RGB channels of a single image file to reduce the number of texture samples needed per pixel in the shader
Procedural material presets in Substance Painter that automatically adapt to mesh properties (curvature, AO, position) to generate realistic surface detail with minimal manual painting

learning_objectives: Build an armature skeleton for a bipedal character with proper bone hierarchy
Configure bone naming conventions and symmetry for mirror operations
Understand forward kinematics and the parent-child bone chain relationship
Set up basic bone constraints for controlled deformation
readings: Blender Documentation: Armatures and Rigging
The Art of Rigging by Kitagawa and Windsor, Ch. 3-5
Rigify Documentation: Understanding the Metarig System

lecture_notes.introduction: Rigging is the process of building the internal skeleton that makes a 3D model move. A rig is to a 3D model what a marionette's strings and control bars are to a puppet: the hidden mechanism that translates the animator's intent into mesh deformation. A good rig makes animation intuitive -- the animator poses the character naturally and the mesh follows smoothly. A bad rig makes every frame a fight against the mesh, producing twisted geometry, collapsed joints, and frustrated animators. Today we build a character rig from scratch, learning the principles that apply regardless of whether you later use auto-rigging tools like Rigify or Mixamo. Understanding manual rigging gives you the knowledge to fix auto-rig problems, customize rigs for specific needs, and build rigs for non-humanoid characters that auto-riggers cannot handle. The rig you build today will drive the animations in your final project.
key_concepts: Armature fundamentals: bones defined by head (root) and tail (tip) positions, arranged in parent-child hierarchies that propagate transformations
Bone hierarchy for bipeds: root bone at hips, spine chain upward (spine, spine.001, spine.002, neck, head), arm chains from upper spine, leg chains from hips
Bone naming conventions: consistent L/R suffix (arm.L, arm.R) enables Blender's symmetrize operations and mirror posing across the X axis
Rest pose vs pose mode: rest pose is the neutral T-pose or A-pose where the rig is built; pose mode applies rotations on top of rest pose for animation
Connected vs free bones: connected bones share parent tail and child head position; free bones have independent head positions for offset chains
Bone display types: octahedral (default), stick (clean viewport), B-Bone (spline visualization), wire (minimal) -- choosing appropriate display for different rig parts
Forward Kinematics (FK): rotating each bone individually from root to tip, giving direct control over every joint angle in the chain
Bone roll: the rotation of a bone around its own axis, critical for correct deformation direction -- must be set consistently across all bones
Custom bone shapes: assigning mesh objects as visual representation of bones for animator-friendly control shapes
Armature creation workflow: start from a single bone, extrude the full chain, subdivide where needed, name everything before adding constraints
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a humanoid armature from scratch: creating the spine chain from root bone at hips through chest, neck, and head; extruding arm chains with upper_arm, forearm, hand bones; extruding leg chains with thigh, shin, foot, toe bones; naming all bones with .L/.R convention; symmetrizing to create the right side; setting correct bone rolls for all chains; and testing FK posing to verify the hierarchy produces natural movement
setup_requirements: Blender with completed and UV-mapped character model from previous sessions
Armature reference diagram (provided: bone placement for humanoid proportions)
Symmetry tools enabled in Edit Mode preferences

review.value: Blender's skeleton system composed of bones in a parent-child hierarchy; transforming a parent bone propagates movement to all children, forming kinematic chains
The parent-child relationships between bones that determine how transformations propagate: rotating the upper arm rotates the forearm and hand automatically
Animating by rotating each bone individually starting from the root, with child bones inheriting parent rotations, giving direct control over every joint angle
The rotation of a bone around its own Y-axis (head-to-tail direction), determining the local X and Z axes used for bone constraints and deformation direction

learning_objectives: Apply automatic weights and understand their limitations for game characters
Manually paint vertex weights to fix deformation problems at joints
Diagnose and resolve common deformation issues: candy wrapper, volume loss, mesh collapse
Use weight paint visualization and editing tools for precise control
readings: Blender Documentation: Weight Painting
Character Animation Fundamentals by Steve Roberts, Ch. 6 (Skinning)
Weight Painting Tips for Game Characters (Polycount wiki)

lecture_notes.introduction: Weight painting is the process of defining how much influence each bone has over each vertex in the mesh. It is the bridge between the skeleton (rig) and the surface (mesh), and its quality determines whether a character deforms beautifully or grotesquely when animated. Automatic weights provide a reasonable starting point by calculating influence based on proximity to bones, but they invariably produce problems at complex joints: shoulders that collapse inward, elbows that lose volume, waists that twist like taffy. Manual weight painting is the craft of fixing these problems and achieving deformation quality that matches professional game assets. Today we learn the weight painting workflow: applying automatic weights as a starting point, identifying deformation problems by posing the rig, and using Blender's weight paint tools to fix each problem area. This is a skill that requires practice and a good eye for anatomy -- understanding where bones, muscles, and skin interact in the real body helps you paint weights that produce natural-looking deformation.
key_concepts: Automatic weights: Blender's bone heat weighting algorithm that assigns initial vertex weights based on proximity to bones, providing a starting point for refinement
Vertex groups: named sets of vertices with influence values (0.0-1.0) corresponding to each bone, defining how much each bone moves each vertex
Weight normalization: ensuring that all bone influences on a single vertex sum to 1.0, preventing vertices from being over-influenced or under-influenced
Weight paint mode: a visualization mode that displays vertex weights as a color gradient (blue=0, green=0.5, red=1.0) for intuitive painting
Brush types: Add (increase weight), Subtract (decrease weight), Mix (blend toward target), Blur (smooth weight boundaries), Gradient (linear weight transitions)
Symmetry painting: mirroring weight changes across the X-axis using the symmetry option, maintaining matching deformation on left and right sides
Common deformation problems: candy wrapper twist (forearm rotation distorts mesh), volume loss at bent joints (elbow pinches), shoulder collapse (arm clips into body)
Corrective techniques: adding intermediate bone influences at problem joints, using smooth gradients rather than hard weight boundaries, extra bones for muscle simulation
Testing deformation: posing the rig at extreme positions (arm raised, knee bent 120 degrees, spine twisted) to stress-test weight painting before animation
Lock and assign: locking vertex groups to prevent accidental modification, using assign/remove to set precise weight values on selected vertices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Weight painting a game character: applying automatic weights with armature parent, entering Pose Mode to bend the arm at 90 degrees, identifying the elbow deformation problem (pinching on the inside, stretching on the outside), entering Weight Paint mode to smooth the transition between upper_arm and forearm weights, fixing the shoulder collapse by painting a gradient that includes the chest bone at the shoulder crease, resolving the candy wrapper twist on the forearm by adding a forearm twist bone with 50% forearm rotation, and testing the full range of motion at all major joints
setup_requirements: Blender with rigged character model from session 6
Weight paint workspace layout with Pose Mode and Weight Paint split view
Deformation test pose set (provided: 8 extreme poses for stress testing)

review.value: The process of assigning how much each bone influences each vertex during deformation, painted as a gradient from 0 (no influence, blue) to 1 (full influence, red) across the mesh surface
Blender's bone heat algorithm that calculates initial vertex weights based on proximity to bones, providing a starting point that requires manual refinement at complex joints
A deformation artifact where rotating a bone along its axis causes the mesh to collapse like a twisted candy wrapper, solved by adding a twist bone with partial rotation inheritance
Ensuring that all bone influences on each vertex sum to exactly 1.0, preventing vertices from being double-transformed or left stationary during animation

learning_objectives: Animate a bipedal walk cycle using keyframe animation with proper timing and spacing
Apply the 12 principles of animation to 3D character movement
Use the Graph Editor to refine animation curves for natural-looking motion
Organize animations as reusable Actions for game engine export
readings: The Animator's Survival Kit by Richard Williams, Ch. 7-9 (Walks)
Blender Documentation: Keyframe Animation and Actions
Animator's Survival Kit Animated App (interactive walk cycle reference)

lecture_notes.introduction: The walk cycle is the animator's Hello World -- deceptively simple in concept, deeply complex in execution. A convincing bipedal walk requires understanding of weight transfer, balance, timing, counter-rotation, and the mechanical reality of how humans maintain forward momentum while alternating between single-leg and double-leg support phases. Every walk cycle has four key positions: contact (feet widest apart), down (weight received on front foot, lowest body position), passing (rear leg passes the support leg), and up (push-off from support foot, highest body position). Between these keys, the animator places breakdowns that define the character of the walk: a confident stride, a tired shuffle, a cautious creep. Today we build a complete walk cycle from blocking (posing the four key positions) through refinement (adding breakdowns, secondary motion, and graph editor polish), using the same workflow used in professional game studios. This cycle will be one of the core animations in your final portfolio.
key_concepts: Walk cycle phases: contact (heel strike, feet widest), down (weight received, body lowest), passing (legs cross, single support), up (push-off, body highest)
Blocking workflow: posing only the four key positions first, reviewing timing before adding detail -- prevents wasted work on bad timing
Breakdown poses: intermediate poses between keys that define the character of movement (arcs, easing, overlap)
Weight and balance: the center of gravity shifts laterally over the support foot during each step, and forward with overall momentum
Counter-rotation: shoulders rotate opposite to hips to maintain balance, with arms swinging opposite to their respective legs
Secondary motion: overlap and follow-through on arms, head bob, clothing movement that trails the primary body motion
Graph Editor: F-curve display showing the value of animated properties over time, enabling precise control of easing and interpolation
Interpolation types: Bezier (smooth default), Linear (constant speed), Constant (instant change) -- and when each is appropriate
Actions: named animation clips that store keyframe data, enabling multiple animations on a single rig without conflict
NLA (Non-Linear Animation): Blender's system for layering, blending, and sequencing Actions on a timeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Animating a 24-frame walk cycle: blocking the 4 key positions (contact left, down left, passing left, up left) with the right side automatically offset by 12 frames for a complete cycle, adding hip sway and vertical bounce, adding shoulder counter-rotation and arm swing with overlap, refining the foot roll from heel contact through toe push-off, polishing timing curves in the Graph Editor to add natural easing at direction changes, checking the cycle loop by setting frame range and enabling looping, and naming the animation as an Action for export
setup_requirements: Blender with rigged and weight-painted character from previous sessions
Animation workspace layout with Timeline, Dope Sheet, and Graph Editor visible
Walk cycle reference video (provided: front, side, and three-quarter views at 24fps)
Auto-keying enabled for efficient posing workflow

review.value: Contact (heel strike, feet widest), Down (weight received, body lowest), Passing (single support, legs cross), Up (push-off, body highest) -- four positions that define the mechanics of bipedal walking
Blender's visual curve editor showing animated property values over time, enabling precise control of easing, interpolation, and timing beyond what is possible with keyframe placement alone
Named animation clips that encapsulate all keyframe data for a specific animation, allowing multiple animations (walk, run, idle) to coexist on a single rig
The principle that shoulders rotate opposite to hips during walking, with arms swinging opposite to their respective legs, maintaining balance and creating natural rhythmic movement

learning_objectives: Create a run cycle that demonstrates increased speed through timing and pose changes
Build a smooth idle animation that keeps characters alive when stationary
Understand the relationship between animation speed and playback rate in game engines
Create transition animations between locomotion states
readings: The Animator's Survival Kit by Richard Williams, Ch. 10-12 (Runs and Timing Variations)
Game Animation Techniques by Steve Swink, Ch. 3
Blender Documentation: NLA Editor

lecture_notes.introduction: A walk cycle teaches the fundamentals, but a game character needs a full locomotion set: idle, walk, run, and transitions between them. Each cycle communicates different energy and intent to the player. A run cycle is not simply a sped-up walk -- it has a fundamentally different mechanical structure. In a walk, one foot is always on the ground (single or double support phase). In a run, there is an airborne phase where both feet are off the ground. This changes the center of gravity behavior, the arm swing amplitude, the body lean, and the vertical bounce frequency. Today we expand from a single walk cycle to a complete locomotion suite, learning how each animation relates to the others and how game engines blend between them based on character speed. We also build the idle animation -- the most subtle and important animation in the set, because it plays whenever the player is not providing input, which is often the majority of gameplay time.
key_concepts: Run cycle vs walk cycle: run includes an airborne phase (both feet off ground), deeper forward lean, wider arm swing, shorter ground contact time
Idle animation design: subtle breathing motion, weight shifting, occasional blinks or fidgets that keep the character alive without being distracting
Seamless looping: ensuring the first and last frames of a cycle are identical for smooth repetition, checking graph editor curves for continuous tangents at loop points
Cycle length conventions: walk typically 24-30 frames, run 12-18 frames, idle 60-120 frames -- faster actions have fewer frames per cycle
Root motion: animating the character's position within the cycle vs keeping the root stationary and moving the game object -- each approach has trade-offs
Transition animations: short clips (8-16 frames) that bridge between states (walk-to-run, run-to-stop, idle-to-walk) for smooth state machine transitions
NLA blending: layering multiple Actions on the NLA timeline with blend modes and influence values for combined animations
Speed scaling: how game engines adjust animation playback rate based on character movement speed, and designing animations that work across a speed range
Foot sliding detection: verifying that foot contact position matches the expected ground plane throughout the cycle to prevent the appearance of skating
Animation libraries and reuse: organizing animations as linkable Actions that can be applied to different characters sharing the same rig structure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a run cycle and idle animation: creating a 16-frame run cycle with airborne phase, deeper forward lean, and wider arm swing than the walk; building a 90-frame idle animation with subtle breathing chest expansion, occasional weight shift, and a head look variation; testing both cycles in looped playback; creating a 10-frame walk-to-run transition animation; and layering walk and run on the NLA timeline to demonstrate blending
setup_requirements: Blender with walk cycle animation from session 8
Run cycle reference video (provided: front and side views)
NLA Editor visible in workspace layout
Frame rate set to 24fps for animation consistency

review.value: The defining mechanical difference between walk and run: during a run, both feet leave the ground simultaneously, creating a flight phase that requires greater energy and forward lean
A looping animation of 60-120 frames showing subtle life signs (breathing, weight shifting, blinking) that plays when the character has no input, preventing a frozen appearance
Animating forward movement within the animation data itself, allowing the game engine to extract displacement from the animation rather than moving the character at a code-defined speed
Short animation clips (8-16 frames) that bridge between locomotion states, providing smooth visual continuity when the character changes from walking to running or stops

learning_objectives: Create facial blend shapes (shape keys) for expression and speech animation
Understand FACS-based facial animation and its application to game characters
Animate lip sync and emotional expressions using shape key drivers
Combine skeletal and shape key animation for full character performance
readings: Stop Staring: Facial Modeling and Animation Done Right by Jason Osipa, Ch. 5-7
Blender Documentation: Shape Keys
FACS (Facial Action Coding System) Reference Guide: Core Action Units

lecture_notes.introduction: Facial animation is the most challenging and rewarding area of character animation. The human face has over 40 muscles that work in combination to produce expressions, and the human brain is extraordinarily sensitive to facial movement -- we can detect a fake smile in milliseconds because the orbicularis oculi muscle around the eyes does not engage the way it does in a genuine smile. In game animation, facial performance is achieved primarily through blend shapes (called shape keys in Blender): morph targets that deform the face mesh from a neutral pose to specific expression poses. The Facial Action Coding System (FACS) provides a scientific framework for decomposing facial expressions into individual muscle actions (Action Units), which map naturally to blend shapes. Today we build a FACS-based facial rig using shape keys, create a library of expressions, and animate a simple dialogue sequence with lip sync and emotional performance.
key_concepts: Shape keys (blend shapes/morph targets): stored mesh deformations that can be blended from 0-1, with multiple shape keys combined additively for complex expressions
FACS Action Units: AU1 (inner brow raise), AU2 (outer brow raise), AU4 (brow lower), AU6 (cheek raise), AU12 (lip corner pull/smile), AU15 (lip corner depress/frown)
Basis shape key: the neutral face pose from which all other shape keys are defined as deformations
Corrective shape keys: blend shapes that fix artifacts from combining two other shapes, driven automatically when both source shapes are active
Visemes: mouth shapes for speech sounds -- common game viseme set: A/I, E, O, U, F/V, M/B/P, L, TH, rest
Shape key drivers: linking shape key values to bone rotations or other properties for automated facial control
Emotional expressions: combining Action Units for anger (AU4+AU5+AU23), sadness (AU1+AU4+AU15), surprise (AU1+AU2+AU5+AU26), disgust (AU9+AU10)
Asymmetric expressions: real faces are never perfectly symmetric; adding L/R variants of shape keys for natural-looking asymmetric expressions
Performance capture vs hand animation: when motion capture data is appropriate and when hand animation gives better artistic control
Blend shape optimization: limiting the number of active shape keys per frame, LOD-based facial animation reduction for distant characters
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a facial rig: creating shape keys for core FACS Action Units (brow raise, brow lower, smile, frown, eye blink, jaw open), creating viseme shape keys for the vowel sounds (A, E, O, U) and key consonants (M, F, L), setting up bone-driven shape keys using custom properties on a control bone, combining shape keys to build an angry expression and a surprised expression, and animating a short 3-second dialogue sequence with lip sync timed to provided audio reference
setup_requirements: Blender with character model that has proper facial topology (edge loops around eyes and mouth)
FACS reference sheet with Action Unit descriptions (provided)
Audio reference clip for lip sync exercise (provided: 3-second dialogue)
Shape Key panel visible in Properties editor

review.value: Stored mesh deformations that morph the base mesh toward a target pose, blendable from 0 to 1, with multiple shapes combinable additively for complex facial expressions
Standardized facial muscle actions from the Facial Action Coding System that decompose expressions into independent components, mapping directly to individual blend shapes
Visual mouth shapes corresponding to speech sounds, typically 10-15 key shapes that cover the phoneme space for lip-sync animation in games
Automated connections that control shape key values based on bone transformations or custom properties, enabling facial control through intuitive rig controls rather than manual slider adjustment

learning_objectives: Optimize high-poly models to game-ready polygon budgets without losing visual quality
Use Blender's Decimate modifier and manual retopology techniques effectively
Understand polygon budget allocation for different asset types and target platforms
Validate mesh quality for game engine compatibility
readings: Real-Time Rendering by Akenine-Moller et al., Ch. 16 (Polygon Techniques)
Blender Documentation: Decimate Modifier and Retopology Tools
Polycount Wiki: Game Art Best Practices (Low Poly Workflows)

lecture_notes.introduction: Game-ready assets must balance visual quality with performance constraints. A film-quality character might use 5 million polygons; a current-gen game character typically uses 20,000-80,000 triangles; a mobile game character might use 2,000-5,000. The art of optimization is removing polygons that contribute nothing visible while preserving every polygon that defines the silhouette and surface detail. This is not just about running a decimation algorithm -- automated tools cannot distinguish between a polygon that defines a character's jawline (critical) and one that adds curvature to the back of the head (expendable at medium distance). Today we learn both automated and manual optimization techniques, polygon budget planning for different platforms and asset types, and the validation process that ensures your optimized mesh imports correctly into game engines without artifacts.
key_concepts: Triangle budgets by platform: mobile (1K-5K tris), VR (5K-15K tris), current-gen console/PC (15K-80K tris per character), environmental props (50-5000 tris depending on screen coverage)
Decimate modifier: Collapse (reduce overall density), Un-Subdivide (reverse subdivision), Planar (remove flat detail) -- each for different optimization scenarios
Manual retopology: building new clean topology over a sculpted or scanned mesh using Shrinkwrap modifier, F2 addon, or RetopoFlow
Silhouette preservation: maintaining polygons that define the object's outline from common viewing angles while removing interior detail
Normal map compensation: baking high-poly detail as normal maps so the low-poly mesh retains visual complexity without geometric cost
LOD (Level of Detail) meshes: creating multiple resolution versions that swap based on camera distance for optimal performance
Mesh validation checklist: no non-manifold geometry, consistent normals, no degenerate triangles, no overlapping vertices, watertight if needed for physics
Hard edges vs smooth shading: using auto-smooth angle and sharp edges to control shading without adding geometry
Vertex count vs triangle count: understanding that vertices are split at UV seams, hard edges, and vertex color boundaries, so the actual vertex count in the GPU is higher than the mesh vertex count
Draw call optimization: combining separate mesh objects that share materials into single meshes to reduce draw call overhead
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optimizing a character model from 50K tris to 8K tris: analyzing the original mesh to identify areas of excessive density, using the Decimate modifier with vertex groups to protect the face and hands while aggressively reducing the body and clothing, manually fixing topology problems introduced by decimation, baking the original detail as a normal map, comparing the high-poly and low-poly models side-by-side in Material Preview to verify visual equivalence, and running the mesh validation checklist
setup_requirements: Blender with high-poly character or prop model from previous sessions
Triangle counter display enabled in viewport overlay
Normal map baking setup from session 5 material

review.value: The maximum polygon count allocated to an asset based on its screen importance, viewing distance, and target platform performance constraints
Multiple resolution versions of the same mesh that automatically swap based on camera distance, using high-poly close up and low-poly at distance to maintain performance
Creating new clean quad-dominant topology over an existing high-poly sculpt or scan, producing an optimized game-ready mesh while preserving the original form
Prioritizing polygons that define an object's outline from typical viewing angles during optimization, since silhouette reads more strongly than surface detail at distance

learning_objectives: Export complete character models with textures and animations to game-ready formats
Understand the differences between FBX and glTF formats and when to use each
Configure export settings for specific game engines (Godot, Unity, Unreal)
Troubleshoot common export and import problems
readings: glTF 2.0 Specification Overview (Khronos Group)
Blender Documentation: FBX Export and glTF Export
Godot Documentation: Importing 3D Assets

lecture_notes.introduction: The export pipeline is where 3D art meets game engineering. A model that looks perfect in Blender can arrive in a game engine with missing textures, broken animations, inverted normals, or mangled bone hierarchies if the export is not configured correctly. The two dominant exchange formats are FBX (Autodesk's proprietary format, the legacy industry standard) and glTF 2.0 (the Khronos Group's open standard, increasingly adopted as the JPEG of 3D). Understanding both formats, their capabilities and limitations, and the correct export settings for your target engine is essential for professional 3D work. Today we take a complete character with textures and animations through both export pipelines, importing into Godot and Unity to verify that everything transfers correctly. We will also build a troubleshooting checklist for the most common export problems and their solutions.
key_concepts: glTF 2.0 (.glb/.gltf): open standard, PBR material support, embedded or external textures, compact binary (.glb) or JSON-readable (.gltf), excellent Blender and Godot support
FBX (.fbx): Autodesk proprietary format, wider legacy support, complex armature hierarchies, the standard for Unity and Unreal Engine workflows
Coordinate system differences: Blender Z-up right-handed vs Unity Y-up left-handed vs Unreal Z-up left-handed -- exporters handle conversion automatically when configured correctly
Scale issues: Blender uses meters by default, some engines expect centimeters -- export scale factor and unit settings prevent tiny or enormous models
Texture embedding: glTF .glb embeds all textures into a single file; FBX can embed but often references external texture files that must be co-located
Animation export: separating multiple Actions into individual animation clips, setting frame ranges per action, enabling NLA strip export for complex setups
Bone orientation issues: FBX bone export may flip axes on import; the 'Primary Bone Axis' and 'Secondary Bone Axis' settings control this behavior
Material conversion: Blender's Principled BSDF maps directly to PBR materials in glTF; FBX materials may need remapping in the target engine
Batch export workflows: scripting export of multiple assets with consistent settings using Blender's Python API for production pipelines
Validation in target engine: importing the exported file, checking material assignment, playing animations, verifying scale, and testing in a simple scene
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exporting a complete character to both formats: configuring glTF export with embedded textures, applying transforms, and selecting animation Actions for export; importing into Godot 4 and verifying materials auto-apply, animations play correctly, and scale matches the game world; configuring FBX export with correct bone axis settings; importing into Unity and remapping materials to URP; comparing the results and documenting any differences; troubleshooting a deliberately broken export (wrong scale, missing textures, flipped normals) to practice diagnosis
setup_requirements: Blender with fully textured, rigged, and animated character from previous sessions
Godot 4 project for import testing
Unity 2022+ project for FBX import testing (optional, Godot is primary)
Export settings reference sheet (provided: per-engine recommendations)

review.value: An open standard 3D format by Khronos Group supporting PBR materials, skeletal animation, and texture embedding in a compact binary (.glb) or readable JSON (.gltf) format
Autodesk's proprietary 3D interchange format with broad engine support, handling complex armatures and animation but requiring careful axis and scale configuration

The automatic transformation between different engine coordinate conventions (Blender Z-up to Unity Y-up) handled by export settings, which must be configured correctly to prevent rotated or flipped models
The unit conversion multiplier applied during export to match the target engine's expected unit system, preventing models from appearing microscopic or enormous on import

learning_objectives: Model modular environment pieces that tile and connect seamlessly
Build a complete game environment from modular asset kits
Apply material variation and weathering to environment surfaces
Optimize environment scenes for real-time rendering performance
readings: The Art of Modular Level Design by Christopher Totten, Ch. 3
World of Level Design: Modular Environment Workflow
Blender Documentation: Geometry Nodes for Scattering

lecture_notes.introduction: Environment modeling for games follows fundamentally different rules than character modeling. Characters are unique, high-detail assets viewed up close. Environments are vast spaces built from repeated modular pieces viewed at varying distances, where the challenge is creating visual variety and atmosphere from a limited set of reusable assets. Modular environment design is an engineering discipline as much as an artistic one: pieces must snap to a consistent grid, connect without visible seams, tile without obvious repetition, and support the level designer's ability to build diverse spaces from a standard kit. Today we learn the modular workflow used in professional studios, from defining grid standards and connection rules to building a complete environment scene from kit pieces. We also explore material techniques for adding visual variety to repeated geometry: vertex color variation, decal layers, and procedural weathering that makes identical meshes look distinct.
key_concepts: Modular grid standards: defining a base unit (1m, 2m, 4m) that all environment pieces align to, ensuring seamless connections
Kit piece categories: floors, walls, corners, pillars, trim pieces, props, hero pieces -- each with defined connection points
Tiling geometry: ensuring wall and floor pieces connect seamlessly at module boundaries with matching edge profiles
Material variation on identical meshes: vertex colors for per-instance tinting, decal meshes for unique damage and stains, parameter randomization
Trim sheets: narrow texture atlases containing repeated architectural details (moldings, rivets, pipes) UV-mapped along strips of geometry
Vertex color for variation: painting color multipliers on vertices so identical meshes can appear weathered, dirty, or tinted differently
Instance rendering: using identical mesh data with different transforms for memory-efficient environment population
Scene composition: arranging assets with attention to focal points, sight lines, lighting, and player flow through the space
Geometry Nodes for scattering: using Blender's node-based system to procedurally place grass, rocks, and debris across terrain surfaces
Performance budgets for environments: balancing draw calls (unique meshes), vertex count, texture memory, and lighting complexity within target frame rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a modular dungeon corridor: defining a 2m grid standard, modeling floor, wall, wall-corner, pillar, and archway kit pieces that snap to the grid, UV-mapping onto a shared trim sheet for efficient texture use, adding vertex color variation so identical wall pieces appear slightly different, assembling a complete L-shaped corridor with lighting, and measuring performance metrics (draw calls, triangle count, texture memory) in Blender's viewport statistics
setup_requirements: Blender with a clean project for environment work
Grid snapping configured to match the chosen module size (2m)
Trim sheet texture (provided: stone dungeon architectural details)
Environment concept art reference (provided)

review.value: A set of interlocking 3D pieces designed on a consistent grid system that snap together to build diverse environments from a limited number of reusable assets
A narrow texture atlas containing repeated architectural details mapped along strips of geometry, enabling high detail on environment surfaces with minimal unique texture data
Per-vertex color data painted onto mesh instances that multiplies with the base texture, allowing identical meshes to appear uniquely weathered, tinted, or aged
Rendering multiple copies of the same mesh using different transformation matrices, sharing mesh and material data in GPU memory for efficient environment population

learning_objectives: Model, texture, and present props that tell stories within game environments
Apply the full asset pipeline from concept to engine-ready export on a new prop
Achieve portfolio-quality rendering and presentation of 3D work
Conduct peer critique of 3D assets using professional evaluation criteria
readings: Creating Game Props in Blender by Abby Crawford (artstation.com/learning)
The Art of Game Worlds by Dave Morris, Ch. 5 (Props and Storytelling)
ArtStation Tips: How to Present Your 3D Work

lecture_notes.introduction: Props are the vocabulary of environmental storytelling. A dented helmet tells a different story than a polished one. A desk covered in coffee-stained papers says something about its occupant that no dialogue could match. In games, props serve multiple functions: they dress environments for visual richness, communicate narrative without text, provide interactive elements for gameplay, and demonstrate the technical range of the art team. Today we bring together every skill from the course -- modeling, topology, UV mapping, texturing, and presentation -- to create a portfolio-quality prop from concept to final presentation. This is also the session where we practice the peer critique process used in professional studios, evaluating work against objective technical criteria (topology quality, texel density, material accuracy) and subjective artistic criteria (silhouette appeal, surface storytelling, environmental fit). Learning to give and receive constructive critique is as important as the technical skills themselves.
key_concepts: Prop storytelling: surface damage, wear patterns, and customization that communicate history, ownership, and world context
Full pipeline execution: concept sketch, blockout, detailed model, UV unwrap, bake, texture, export, engine validation in a single workflow
Presentation rendering: HDRI lighting in Blender, turntable camera animation, clay/wireframe overlay renders for portfolio display
Material storytelling: PBR values that communicate age (increased roughness), use (wear on grip areas), damage (metallic showing through paint)
Viewport composition: camera angle, lighting, background, and framing that present the prop at its most appealing for portfolio screenshots
Wireframe overlay renders: displaying polygon topology as a semi-transparent wireframe over the textured model to demonstrate technical skill
Professional critique vocabulary: silhouette, read (clarity at distance), value range, material separation, scale reference, edge density distribution
Portfolio presentation standards: 3-5 renders per prop (beauty shot, wireframe, UV layout, flat texture, in-context scene placement)
Turntable animation: 5-10 second 360-degree camera orbit showing all sides of the asset, rendered at 1080p or higher
Engine screenshot: the final validation of showing the asset in its target game engine under gameplay lighting conditions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete prop pipeline in a single session: speed-modeling a fantasy weapon from concept sketch (45 minutes), UV unwrapping with seams along weapon edges and grip boundary, texturing in Substance Painter with metal blade, leather grip, and gemstone materials, exporting to Godot with glTF, setting up a turntable camera rig in Blender for portfolio render, and capturing beauty, wireframe, UV layout, and flat texture screenshots for portfolio presentation
setup_requirements: Blender with full tool access
Substance Painter or Blender Texture Paint mode
Concept reference (provided: fantasy weapon concept sheet)
Turntable camera rig template (provided: Blender file with animated camera)

review.value: Using surface detail (wear, damage, customization, staining) to communicate the history, ownership, and world context of a game prop without relying on text or dialogue
A 360-degree camera orbit animation showing all sides of a 3D asset, standard for portfolio presentation, typically 5-10 seconds with even HDRI lighting
Industry expectations for showing 3D work: beauty render, wireframe overlay, UV layout screenshot, flat texture display, and in-context scene placement
Structured evaluation of 3D work using objective criteria (topology quality, texel density) and subjective criteria (silhouette appeal, material storytelling, environmental fit)

learning_objectives: Curate course work into a cohesive professional portfolio with consistent presentation quality
Create turntable renders, wireframe overlays, and beauty shots for all major assets
Present work to peers with clear articulation of design decisions and technical process
Evaluate portfolio readiness for job applications and further academic work
readings: Art Director's Advice on Portfolio Presentation (GDC 2019 talk notes)
Blender Documentation: Rendering and Compositing
ArtStation Best Practices for Game Artist Portfolios

lecture_notes.introduction: Your portfolio is the single most important factor in getting hired as a 3D artist. Studios evaluate candidates primarily through their portfolio, not their resume. A strong portfolio demonstrates technical range (modeling, texturing, rigging, animation), consistent quality, professional presentation, and the ability to work within production constraints. Today we assemble your best course work into a portfolio-ready collection, ensure consistent presentation quality across all pieces, and practice presenting your work verbally -- a skill needed for studio interviews. The portfolio review also serves as a final course assessment: each piece demonstrates mastery of specific skills, and the collection as a whole demonstrates your growth from week 1 to week 15. Quality over quantity is the cardinal rule: three excellent pieces beat ten mediocre ones. Every asset in your portfolio should represent your best capability, and any piece that falls below your current standard should be reworked or removed.
key_concepts: Portfolio curation: selecting your 4-6 strongest pieces that demonstrate range (character, prop, environment, animation) and consistent quality
Consistent presentation: matching lighting, rendering settings, image dimensions, and background style across all portfolio pieces for professional cohesion
Render settings for portfolio: Cycles or EEVEE at 1920x1080 minimum, HDRI lighting, compositing passes for DOF and color grading
Process documentation: including wireframe, UV layout, and flat texture screenshots to demonstrate technical skill behind the final beauty render
Video reel: compiling turntable renders and animation clips into a 1-2 minute showreel with professional pacing and music
ArtStation and portfolio website: publishing work on industry-standard platforms with proper tagging, descriptions, and process breakdowns
Verbal presentation skills: explaining design decisions, technical challenges, and creative reasoning clearly in 3-5 minutes per piece
Accepting and applying critique: responding to feedback professionally, identifying actionable improvements, and planning portfolio iteration
Resume integration: connecting portfolio pieces to specific skills listed on the resume (PBR texturing, character rigging, modular environment design)
Continuous improvement plan: identifying weak areas and planning practice projects to address them after the course ends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live portfolio assembly: selecting the 5 best pieces from a student's course work, rendering consistent beauty shots with a standardized HDRI setup, assembling wireframe overlays and UV layout screenshots, compiling turntable animations into a 90-second video reel, uploading to ArtStation with descriptive text, and presenting the portfolio to the class with a timed 5-minute verbal walkthrough
setup_requirements: Blender with all course project files accessible
HDRI lighting setup (provided: 3 studio HDRIs for consistent rendering)
Video editing software for reel compilation (DaVinci Resolve free edition recommended)
ArtStation account for publishing

review.value: Selecting your strongest 4-6 pieces that demonstrate range and consistent quality; quality over quantity is the cardinal rule of portfolio building
Including technical breakdowns (wireframe, UV layout, texture flats) alongside beauty renders to demonstrate the skill behind the final presentation
A 1-2 minute video compiling turntable renders and animation clips, demonstrating the full range of work with professional pacing and presentation
The ability to explain design decisions, technical process, and creative reasoning clearly in a time-limited format, essential for studio interviews and portfolio reviews

quizzes.topic: Modeling Fundamentals, Topology, and UV Mapping

questions.question: Explain why quad-dominant topology is preferred over triangles and n-gons for character models that will be animated. Describe what happens to an n-gon at a character's elbow joint when the arm bends 90 degrees.
questions.answer: Quads are preferred because they subdivide predictably (each quad splits into 4 quads), deform smoothly along edge loops, and produce clean results with Subdivision Surface modifiers. Triangles create pinching artifacts at subdivision and have unpredictable deformation direction. N-gons are worse because they must be internally triangulated by the renderer, and this triangulation can change frame-to-frame during animation. At an elbow bent 90 degrees, an n-gon would collapse unpredictably: the internal triangulation might flip, causing visible popping artifacts, surface shading errors, and potential self-intersection. Proper topology uses parallel edge loops running perpendicular to the bend axis so each quad deforms in a controlled accordion-like fashion.

rubric.key: Explains quad advantages for subdivision and deformation
Describes n-gon triangulation problem during animation
Explains correct edge loop orientation at joints

questions.question: When UV unwrapping a game character, where should seams be placed and why? List three specific guidelines for seam placement on a humanoid model.
questions.answer: UV seams should be placed in locations that minimize visual disruption. Three guidelines: (1) Place seams along natural boundaries where materials or clothing change, such as the shirt sleeve edge or waistband, because the eye expects a visual break there. (2) Place seams in areas hidden from the primary camera angle: inner arm, behind ears, shoe sole, inside clothing folds. (3) Place seams along hard geometric edges where there is already a visible crease, such as under the chin or at the armpit, so the seam's texture discontinuity blends with the existing edge. Seams should never cross the center of a large visible smooth surface like the face or chest.

rubric.key: States seams minimize visual disruption as primary goal
Lists three valid, specific placement guidelines with examples
Mentions where seams should NOT go

questions.question: What is texel density and why is it important for game assets? Describe how you would verify and correct inconsistent texel density on a UV-unwrapped model.
questions.answer: Texel density is the number of texture pixels per unit of 3D surface area. It is important because inconsistent texel density causes visible quality differences across the model: areas with low texel density appear blurry while adjacent areas with high density appear sharp, breaking visual cohesion. To verify: apply a checker pattern texture and inspect in Material Preview -- squares that appear uniformly sized across the model indicate consistent density; stretched or compressed squares indicate problem areas. To correct: in the UV Editor, scale UV islands so that the checker appears uniform -- typically the face and hands get proportionally more UV space (higher priority detail) while the back and inner surfaces get less. The overall target density depends on texture resolution and model size.

rubric.key: Correct definition of texel density with visual quality rationale
Describes checker pattern verification method
Explains correction by UV island scaling with priority areas mentioned

questions.question: List the standard PBR texture channels in a metallic/roughness workflow and describe what each encodes. A rusty iron fence has both bare metal and rust patches -- describe the approximate Metallic and Roughness values for each surface type and explain why.
questions.answer: PBR channels: Base Color (surface color/albedo without lighting), Metallic (0=non-metal/dielectric, 1=metal), Roughness (0=mirror-smooth, 1=completely rough), Normal (surface detail as tangent-space vectors in RGB), AO (pre-baked contact shadows), Emissive (self-illumination). For the iron fence: bare metal areas have Metallic ~1.0 (iron is a metal) and Roughness ~0.3-0.5 (polished but not mirror-smooth). Rust areas have Metallic ~0.0 (rust is iron oxide, a non-metallic compound) and Roughness ~0.8-1.0 (very rough matte surface). Base Color also changes: silvery-gray for bare metal, orange-brown for rust. The key insight is that rust is chemically a different material from iron, so its PBR values are fundamentally different despite being on the same object.

rubric.key: Lists at least 5 PBR channels with correct descriptions
Correct Metallic values with explanation (metal ~1.0, rust ~0.0 because oxide is non-metal)
Correct Roughness values with reasoning (metal smooth, rust rough)

questions.question: Compare Forward Kinematics (FK) and Inverse Kinematics (IK) for character animation. For a walk cycle, which approach is better for the legs and why? Name one animation task where FK is clearly superior to IK.
questions.answer: FK rotates each bone individually from root to tip, with child bones inheriting parent rotations. IK positions the end effector (hand, foot) and the solver calculates all parent bone rotations automatically. For walk cycle legs, IK is superior because feet must plant at precise ground positions -- the animator places the foot where it contacts the ground, and hip/knee angles are calculated automatically. This prevents foot sliding, the most common walk cycle error. FK is superior for arms during expressive gestures like a sweeping wave or swinging arms while walking, because FK gives direct arc control at each joint. IK arm movement tends to look linear and mechanical because the solver finds the shortest path rather than natural arcs.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both IK and FK with clear distinction
Explains why IK is better for legs (foot planting, preventing sliding)
Names a valid FK-superior scenario with arc control reasoning

questions.question: Explain the 'candy wrapper twist' deformation artifact. What causes it, and describe two different solutions for fixing it in a character rig.
questions.answer: The candy wrapper twist occurs when a bone rotates along its own axis (twist/roll) and the mesh collapses around the bone like a twisted candy wrapper. This is most visible in the forearm during wrist rotation and in the upper arm during shoulder roll. It happens because single-bone influence creates a hard transition between influenced and non-influenced vertices during axial rotation. Solution 1: Add a twist bone (forearm_twist.L) that inherits only a percentage (typically 50%) of the forearm's axial rotation, distributing the twist over a longer section of mesh and softening the deformation. Solution 2: Add a corrective shape key (blend shape) driven by the bone's rotation angle that reshapes the mesh to maintain volume at extreme twist angles, countering the collapse programmatically.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the artifact and its visual appearance
Explains the cause (single-bone axial rotation, hard weight transition)
Describes two valid solutions with specific implementation details

questions.question: Name the four key positions in a walk cycle and describe the body mechanics at each position. Explain the role of counter-rotation between hips and shoulders during a walk.
questions.answer: Four positions: (1) Contact -- the moment of heel strike when feet are widest apart, arms at maximum swing, body at mid-height. (2) Down -- weight transfers to the front foot, body drops to its lowest point, front knee bends to absorb impact, opposite arm begins to swing back. (3) Passing -- the rear leg passes the support leg, body is on single-leg support, hips tilt toward the swing leg side, body at mid-height. (4) Up -- push-off from the support foot toe, body at its highest point, back leg fully extended. Counter-rotation: when the right leg steps forward the right hip rotates forward, and to maintain balance the right shoulder rotates backward (left arm swings forward). This opposing rotation keeps the center of gravity stable and is the signature of natural walking -- its absence makes walks look robotic.

rubric.key: All four positions named with correct body mechanics
Explains counter-rotation between hips and shoulders
Explains why counter-rotation is necessary (balance, natural appearance)

questions.question: What is the difference between a walk cycle and a run cycle in terms of body mechanics? Specifically address the airborne phase and how it changes the timing and pose requirements.
questions.answer: The fundamental mechanical difference is that in a walk, at least one foot is always on the ground, while in a run, there is an airborne phase where both feet are off the ground simultaneously. This changes several things: timing is faster (run cycles are typically 12-18 frames vs 24-30 for walks), the forward lean is greater to maintain momentum through the flight phase, arm swing amplitude increases for balance, the contact position shows the foot striking further forward, the down position absorbs more impact force (deeper knee bend), and the push-off is more explosive. The airborne phase also means the center of gravity follows a more pronounced arc (higher vertical bounce) and the body spends less time in ground contact relative to the cycle length.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the airborne phase as the key difference
Describes at least 3 specific changes (lean, arm swing, timing, bounce)
Mentions timing difference with approximate frame counts

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the Graph Editor in Blender animation. Why would an animator need to edit interpolation curves rather than just placing keyframes? Describe the difference between Bezier and Linear interpolation and when each is appropriate.
questions.answer: The Graph Editor displays animated property values as curves over time (F-curves), enabling precise control of timing, spacing, and easing that is impossible with keyframe placement alone. Keyframes define the value at specific frames, but the interpolation between them determines how the value changes -- which is equally important for animation quality. Bezier interpolation creates smooth acceleration and deceleration using tangent handles, producing natural-looking motion where objects ease into and out of key positions (a bouncing ball slows at the top of its arc). Linear interpolation creates constant speed between keyframes, producing mechanical motion appropriate for machines, conveyor belts, or clock hands. Using linear where Bezier is needed makes organic motion look robotic; using Bezier where linear is needed makes mechanical motion look organic and wrong.

rubric.key: Explains Graph Editor purpose for controlling motion between keyframes
Correctly describes Bezier (smooth easing) and Linear (constant speed)
Gives appropriate use cases for each interpolation type

questions.question: Explain the FACS (Facial Action Coding System) approach to facial animation. What are Action Units, and why is decomposing expressions into individual muscle actions better than creating whole-expression blend shapes like 'happy' or 'angry'?
questions.answer: FACS decomposes facial expressions into individual muscle actions called Action Units (AUs). Examples: AU1 (inner brow raise), AU4 (brow lower), AU6 (cheek raise), AU12 (lip corner pull). Creating individual AU blend shapes instead of whole expressions is superior because expressions are combinations of AUs -- anger is AU4+AU5+AU23, surprise is AU1+AU2+AU5+AU26. With individual AUs, the animator can create any expression by combining shapes, including subtle transitions and mixed emotions (a sad smile uses both smile AU12 and brow lower AU4). With whole-expression blend shapes, you can only blend between predefined expressions, and combining 'happy' and 'angry' produces averaged mush rather than a meaningful expression. FACS also maps directly to performance capture data, making the transition from recorded data to animation straightforward.

rubric.key: Correctly defines Action Units with specific examples
Explains combinatorial advantage over whole-expression shapes
Mentions mixed emotions or subtle transitions as a benefit

questions.question: A character model is 50,000 triangles and needs to be optimized to 8,000 for a mobile game. Describe a systematic approach to this optimization, including which areas to prioritize for reduction and how to compensate for lost geometric detail.
questions.answer: Systematic approach: (1) Analyze the mesh to identify polygon density distribution -- often the body, clothing folds, and interior surfaces have excessive detail invisible at game camera distance. (2) Use vertex groups to protect critical areas: face (highest priority, most visible), hands (medium priority), silhouette-defining edges. (3) Apply the Decimate modifier with Un-Subdivide or Collapse mode, targeting protected and unprotected regions separately. (4) Manually fix topology problems introduced by decimation: reconnect edge loops at joints, restore quad topology in animation-critical areas. (5) Compensate for lost detail by baking a normal map from the 50K version onto the 8K version, capturing all surface detail as texture data. (6) Bake AO and curvature maps for additional surface information. (7) Validate: check deformation at all joints, verify silhouette from common angles, test in-engine at target camera distance to confirm no visible quality loss.

rubric.key: Systematic approach with priority areas identified (face, hands, silhouette)
Uses both automated (Decimate) and manual topology cleanup
Compensates with normal map baking and validates in-engine

questions.question: Compare glTF 2.0 and FBX export formats for game assets. When would you choose each? Describe one common export problem and how to diagnose and fix it.
questions.answer: glTF 2.0 is an open standard by Khronos Group, excellent for Godot and web (three.js), with native PBR material support and compact binary .glb format that embeds textures. FBX is Autodesk's proprietary format, the standard for Unity and Unreal, with broader legacy support and better handling of complex armature hierarchies.
