Choose glTF for Godot, web targets, and when you want an open format with guaranteed material compatibility. Choose FBX for Unity/Unreal workflows and when the pipeline requires Autodesk tool compatibility. Common problem: model imports at wrong scale (appears tiny or enormous). Diagnosis: check if a 1m cube in Blender appears as 1 unit in the engine. Fix: in Blender export settings, verify 'Apply Transform' is checked, and set the scale factor to match the engine's unit system (FBX export to Unity needs scale 1.0 with 'Apply Scalings: FBX All').

rubric.key: Correctly describes both formats with appropriate use cases
Identifies a valid common export problem
Provides diagnosis method and specific fix with export settings

quizzes.topic: Environment Art, Props, and Portfolio Presentation

questions.question: Explain the modular kit approach to environment art. What is a grid standard, and why must all kit pieces align to it? Describe how you would add visual variety to a corridor built from identical wall modules.
questions.answer: Modular kit design creates a limited set of interlocking 3D pieces on a consistent grid that snap together to build diverse environments. A grid standard defines the base unit dimensions (e.g., 2m x 2m x 3m) that all pieces align to, ensuring modules connect seamlessly without gaps or overlaps. Without a grid standard, level designers spend hours manually adjusting piece positions, and seams appear at every connection. For visual variety on identical wall modules: (1) Vertex color painting to tint individual instances with slight color variation (warmer/cooler, lighter/darker). (2) Decal meshes overlaid on walls for unique damage, graffiti, water stains, or moss. (3) Prop placement in front of walls (torches, banners, shelves) that break up the repetition. (4) Rotating and flipping modules to change the pattern orientation. (5) Material parameter randomization per instance for slight roughness and color shifts.

rubric.key: Explains modular kit concept and grid standard purpose
Explains why grid alignment is necessary (seamless connections, designer efficiency)
Describes at least 3 techniques for visual variety on repeated modules

questions.question: What are the essential components of a professional 3D art portfolio for a game artist position? List the renders/screenshots that should accompany each asset, and explain why wireframe and UV layout images are included alongside beauty renders.
questions.answer: A professional game art portfolio should include 4-6 strongest pieces showing range (character, prop, environment, animation reel). Each asset should have: (1) Beauty render with proper lighting and composition showing the final textured model. (2) Wireframe overlay render showing polygon topology on the textured surface. (3) UV layout screenshot showing island arrangement and space utilization. (4) Flat texture sheets showing Base Color, Normal, and Roughness maps. (5) In-context screenshot showing the asset in a game engine scene. Wireframe and UV images are included because art directors evaluate technical skill, not just artistic result. Clean topology demonstrates the artist can create assets that animate and perform well. Efficient UV layouts show the artist understands texture memory budgets. These technical images separate professional game artists from hobbyists who can produce good renders but cannot create production-ready assets.

rubric.key: Lists portfolio components with range requirement (4-6 pieces, diverse types)
Names at least 4 render types per asset
Explains why technical images matter for hiring (production readiness, technical skill evaluation)

questions.question: Describe trim sheets and explain how they differ from standard UV-mapped textures. Why are trim sheets particularly efficient for environment art, and what types of environmental details are they best suited for?
questions.answer: A trim sheet is a narrow texture atlas containing repeated architectural details (moldings, rivets, panel lines, pipes, ornamental trim) arranged in horizontal or vertical strips. Unlike standard UV mapping where each face maps to a unique area of the texture, trim sheet UVs map geometry strips to the appropriate detail band on the trim texture, reusing the same texture data across many different meshes. Trim sheets are efficient for environments because: multiple different wall, floor, and ceiling meshes can share a single trim texture, drastically reducing unique texture count and memory usage. They are best suited for repeating linear details: stone moldings on walls, metal rivets along structural beams, decorative borders on floors, pipes running along ceilings, panel line details on sci-fi surfaces. They are NOT suited for large unique surface areas (a character face) or features that need to appear at many different scales.

rubric.key: Correctly defines trim sheets with the strip/band UV mapping concept
Explains efficiency advantage (shared texture, reduced memory, multiple meshes)
Names appropriate detail types and limitations

major_assignments.title: Game-Ready Character Model
major_assignments.description: Create a complete game-ready character model in Blender: modeled with clean quad topology optimized for animation, UV unwrapped with efficient island packing and consistent texel density, textured with a full PBR material set, and rigged with a functional skeleton with weight-painted deformation. The character must import cleanly into Godot or Unity.
objectives: Demonstrate clean polygon modeling with proper edge flow for animation deformation
UV unwrap with strategically hidden seams and consistent texel density verified by checker pattern
Create a complete PBR texture set (Base Color, Metallic, Roughness, Normal, AO) with physically accurate values
Rig with a functional armature and weight paint clean deformation at all major joints
deliverables: Blender source file (.blend) with complete character model, rig, and materials
Exported glTF file (.glb) ready for game engine import
Texture files at 2048x2048 resolution (PNG format: Base Color, Normal, ORM channel-packed)
Turntable render video (15 seconds, 360-degree rotation) with proper lighting
Wireframe overlay renders from front, side, and three-quarter views
UV layout screenshot showing island arrangement and texel density

criteria: Clean quad-dominant topology with no n-gons in deformation areas
Edge loops follow anatomical form and support animation at all major joints
Polygon count appropriate for target platform (3000-8000 triangles)
No mesh errors: no non-manifold edges, no zero-area faces, consistent normals
UV seams placed in hidden or natural boundary locations
Texel density consistent across model verified by checker pattern
PBR textures physically accurate with correct metallic/roughness values per material type
Normal map baking clean with no projection artifacts
Armature has proper bone hierarchy with consistent naming conventions
IK chains functional for legs with proper pole targets
Weight painting produces clean deformation at all major joints through full range of motion
Character achieves basic poses (T-pose, walking contact, sitting) without mesh breakage
Turntable render well-lit and professionally presented
Wireframe renders clearly show topology decisions
glTF export imports correctly into Godot without errors
File organized with clear naming for all objects, materials, and textures
Professional-quality game character with clean, animation-ready topology
Complete PBR texturing with physically accurate material values
Functional rig with clean deformation verified at extreme poses
Portfolio-quality presentation renders and clean engine-compatible export

major_assignments.title: Animated Character Showcase
major_assignments.description: Using the rigged character from Assignment 1, create a complete set of game-ready animations: a walk cycle, a run cycle, an idle animation, and one action animation of your choice (attack, jump, interact, emote). All animations must loop seamlessly where appropriate and export correctly as separate Actions in glTF format.
objectives: Animate a convincing bipedal walk cycle with proper weight transfer, timing, and counter-rotation
Create a run cycle that demonstrates correct airborne phase mechanics
Build a subtle idle animation that keeps the character alive when stationary
Demonstrate the 12 principles of animation applied to 3D character performance
Export all animations as separate game-engine-compatible Actions
deliverables: Blender file with all animations as separate named Actions
Exported glTF file (.glb) with embedded animations
Render videos of each animation from front and three-quarter views
Animation breakdown document showing key poses, timing charts, and reference material

criteria: Walk cycle has correct phases: contact, down, passing, up with proper timing
Run cycle includes airborne phase with appropriate forward lean and arm swing
Both locomotion cycles loop seamlessly with no popping or foot sliding
All animations export correctly to glTF with proper frame ranges
Believable weight distribution and center of gravity shift in both cycles
Hip rotation and shoulder counter-rotation present and correctly timed
Idle animation feels alive with subtle breathing and weight shifting without distraction
Action animation demonstrates clear anticipation, action, and recovery phases
No mesh deformation artifacts visible during any animation
Graph Editor curves are clean with appropriate interpolation at all keyframes
At least 3 animation principles demonstrated across the full set (overlap, arcs, squash/stretch, etc.)
Animation breakdown document shows analytical understanding of locomotion mechanics
Key pose diagrams included for walk and run cycles
Timing charts show frame numbers for all key poses
Reference material cited and analyzed
Render videos clearly show the quality of animation from multiple angles
Walk and run cycles demonstrate strong understanding of locomotion mechanics
All animations apply classical animation principles to 3D character performance
Technical execution is clean with no deformation artifacts or export issues
Thorough documentation demonstrates analytical understanding of the animation process

major_assignments.title: Environment Kit and Portfolio Compilation
major_assignments.description: Create a modular environment kit (minimum 8 unique pieces) with shared materials and a trim sheet, assemble a complete scene from the kit, and compile your best course work into a portfolio-ready package with consistent presentation quality including turntable renders, wireframe overlays, and a video showreel.
objectives: Design and model a modular environment kit with consistent grid standards
Apply trim sheet texturing and vertex color variation for visual diversity
Assemble a complete scene demonstrating the kit's versatility
Compile a professional portfolio package with consistent presentation across all pieces
deliverables: Blender file with modular kit pieces and assembled scene
Trim sheet texture (2048x2048) with documentation of strip contents
Scene render from 3 camera angles with proper lighting
Portfolio package: beauty renders, wireframe overlays, and UV layouts for all major course pieces
Video showreel (60-90 seconds) compiling turntable renders and animation clips
Portfolio website or ArtStation page with all work published

criteria: At least 8 modular kit pieces that connect seamlessly on a defined grid
Trim sheet used effectively with clean UV mapping along detail strips
Vertex color variation creates visual diversity on repeated modules
Scene assembly demonstrates kit versatility with at least 2 distinct area compositions
Environment has cohesive art direction with consistent style and material quality
Lighting enhances scene composition with focal points and mood
Scene renders are portfolio-quality with proper camera composition
All portfolio pieces have consistent presentation (matching lighting, dimensions, background)
Kit pieces use efficient polygon budgets appropriate for environment art
Shared materials and instances used for performance optimization
Video showreel has professional pacing and demonstrates full range of skills
Portfolio represents quality over quantity with curated selection
Grid standard and connection rules documented with diagrams
Trim sheet contents documented with strip identification
Each portfolio piece includes wireframe and UV layout alongside beauty renders
ArtStation or portfolio page is professionally organized with descriptions
Versatile modular kit demonstrating professional environment art workflow
Assembled scene showcases kit with cohesive art direction and lighting
Portfolio compilation shows growth and consistent quality across all skill areas
Presentation quality meets professional standards for job applications

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) assessing 3D modeling concepts, terminology, and technical knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on Blender exercises building specific modeling, texturing, and animation skills

major_assignments.description: Three major projects: Game-Ready Character (150pts), Animated Showcase (150pts), Environment Kit and Portfolio (170pts)

midterm_examination.description: Midterm practical exam: timed modeling and texturing task from reference images

final_project.description: Final portfolio presentation with curated work, video showreel, and verbal presentation to peers

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and critique days. More than 2 unexcused lab absences may result in a full letter grade reduction. Critique participation is graded.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day for up to 5 days. Blender files that do not open or contain missing textures receive an automatic 30% penalty. Always use File > External Data > Pack Resources before submission.
course_policies.collaboration: All models and animations must be your own work. Reference images and tutorials may be used for learning but copying topology or animation keyframes from another source is plagiarism. Cite all references in your submission.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting assets from online marketplaces, asset stores, or AI-generated 3D content as your own work is a violation. All submitted work must be created by you in Blender during this course. AI tools may be used for reference image generation only, with disclosure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a computer with Blender 4.0+, a three-button mouse, and a dedicated GPU (NVIDIA or AMD with at least 4GB VRAM recommended). Lab computers with Blender, Substance Painter, and drawing tablets are available during lab hours.
